context: Next.js 14 + Supabase + TypeScript strict. 30K creator TikTok Shop affiliate management. Previous fixes: API auth, timing-safe comparison, search sanitization, field whitelisting, pagination clamping, commission_rate unit fix, video_id→tiktok_video_id, null guards.

findings.title: /api/kpi PUT endpoint has no authentication and exposes aggregated creator data
trace: PUT /api/kpi receives no auth check (no middleware, no token validation)
Fetches ALL creators from database into memory (line 50: .select('tier, status, source, monthly_gmv', {count: 'exact'}))
Filters and aggregates data in JavaScript (lines 58-76)
Returns aggregated stats including GMV breakdown, tier distribution by creator count, and source breakdown
RLS policies marked as 'authenticated USING (true)' means any logged-in user can read all creators, but service_role key (line 13 in supabase.ts) bypasses RLS entirely
findings.scenario: Given an unauthenticated attacker, when they call PUT /api/kpi, then they can retrieve aggregated data on all creators (total GMV, tier distribution, source breakdown). An authenticated user with minimal privileges could also call this endpoint since middleware doesn't validate authorization level.
findings.impact: Data breach: Sensitive business metrics exposed (total GMV ~$X, creator source distribution revealing recruitment strategy, tier distribution). No audit trail. Public could discover 30K target before official launch.
findings.fix: Add authentication check and authorization validation. Restrict to admin users. Implement Supabase RLS properly instead of 'USING (true)'. Cache aggregated results if needed rather than fetching all creators on each request. Consider moving calculation to scheduled task.

findings.title: /api/shipping POST update_status accepts arbitrary fields without validation
trace: POST body.action === 'update_status' builds updates object from request (lines 109-122)
Only status and related timestamp fields are conditionally added, but field is not whitelisted
Lines 109-112: Object.assign-style allows any request field to be passed as update
No validation that status value is from enum sample_status
No auth check on this endpoint (only on GET)
findings.scenario: Given an authenticated user, when they POST /api/shipping with {action: 'update_status', shipment_id: '...', status: 'invalid_status', extra_field: 'malicious'}, then the request may accept invalid status values or update columns not intended.
findings.impact: Data integrity violation: Invalid shipment status values in database. Potential for adding unexpected fields to sample_shipments table if schema changes allow it. No audit trail of who updated what.
findings.fix: Validate status value against enum, use field whitelist for update object, add authorization check, add audit logging.

findings.title: /api/join POST has no rate limiting on public endpoint
trace: Public endpoint (no authentication required, middleware line 20 in middleware.ts allows /api/join)
POST endpoint accepts JSON with email, tiktok_handle, competitor_experience array
Inserts directly into join_applications table with no rate limit or CAPTCHA
No throttling per IP address, per email, or per tiktok_handle
Could be abused to spam join applications, enumerate creators, or conduct DOS attacks
findings.scenario: Given an attacker, when they script 10,000 POST requests to /api/join with different emails/handles, then the database becomes polluted with fake applications and the team wastes time reviewing spam.
findings.impact: Database pollution, spam, potential DOS, no audit trail of attack source. If application review is manual, attackers waste team time. Email field could be used for mail bombing if notification system sends confirmations.
findings.fix: Implement rate limiting per IP address (e.g., 5 applications per hour), add CAPTCHA validation, consider email verification, add abuse detection (check for duplicate fields, impossible follower counts).

findings.title: Supabase RLS policies use 'USING (true)' allowing ANY authenticated user to read/write ALL data
trace: schema.sql lines 363-376: All table policies grant 'authenticated USING (true)' — means any logged-in user can perform ALL operations
Example: 'CREATE POLICY "Authenticated users can manage creators" ON creators FOR ALL TO authenticated USING (true) WITH CHECK (true);'
schema-v3.sql lines 50, 58: Referral code and referral policies also use 'USING (true)' as catch-all
This defeats RLS security model entirely for authenticated users
createServerClient() in supabase.ts uses service_role key which bypasses RLS anyway, but client-side usage would also be compromised
findings.scenario: Given a low-privilege dashboard user (e.g., intern), when they authenticate and access the frontend or make API requests using supabase client, they can read any creator's data (GMV, emails, payment info) and modify any data.
findings.impact: Privilege escalation: A junior team member with read-only intent could modify creator tier, commission rate, or referral tracking. No authorization enforcement. Violates principle of least privilege.
findings.fix: Replace 'USING (true)' with specific conditions. Implement role-based RLS. Use trigger/RPC to validate user role (admin vs manager vs viewer).
findings.fix_code:
Replace generic policies with granular ones:
```sql
-- Service role (backend API) has full access
CREATE POLICY "Service role full access" ON creators 
  FOR ALL TO service_role USING (true) WITH CHECK (true);

-- Admins can read/write all
CREATE POLICY "Admins manage" ON creators 
  FOR ALL TO authenticated 
  USING (auth.uid() IN (SELECT user_id FROM admins)) 
  WITH CHECK (auth.uid() IN (SELECT user_id FROM admins));

-- Viewers can only read (no write)
CREATE POLICY "Viewers read only" ON creators 
  FOR SELECT TO authenticated 
  USING (auth.uid() IN (SELECT user_id FROM viewers));
```

findings.title: createServerClient() uses service_role key which bypasses ALL RLS policies
trace: Line 10: createServerClient() creates client with SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY
Service role key is documented in Supabase as bypassing RLS for security operations
Used in all API routes (kpi, shipping, join, recommendations, etc.)
While necessary for backend operations, it means RLS is not enforced on those routes
If any API route has authorization bypass, all data is exposed
findings.scenario: Given that /api/kpi has no auth check (SEC-1) and uses createServerClient(), when an attacker calls PUT /api/kpi, they bypass both RLS and app-level auth to fetch all creator data.
findings.impact: Service role key + missing auth checks = complete RLS bypass. Combined with SEC-1 (no /api/kpi auth), this is critical data breach vector.
findings.fix: Add application-level authorization checks in every API route. Don't rely on RLS alone. Implement token validation before calling Supabase. Add audit logging for sensitive operations.

findings.title: /api/kpi PUT route assumes latestKpi.data is always available (potential null dereference)
trace: Line 53: Fetches latest KPI with .single() but no null check
Line 81: Accesses kpi?.weekly_net_increase, kpi?.weeks_to_30k assuming kpi exists
If weekly_kpis table is empty, latestKpi.data is null and lines 86-87 return 0 as default (OK)
However, if latestKpi.error is non-null (e.g., database error), kpi is undefined and ?.chaining works but is sloppy
Line 76: totalGMV calculation uses c.monthly_gmv which could be null or NaN if monthly_gmv is not properly initialized
findings.scenario: Given a fresh database with no KPI records, when PUT /api/kpi is called, then latestKpi.error exists and kpi is undefined. The endpoint still works due to optional chaining but error handling is weak.
findings.impact: Silent failures: If a database error occurs, the endpoint doesn't report it properly. The kpi?.optional_prop || 0 pattern masks real errors. Weak error handling makes debugging harder.
findings.fix: Explicitly check for error, handle null kpi, add logging.

findings.title: Loose type casting with 'any' in /api/kpi PUT and filter operations
trace: Lines 56, 58, 61-64, 68-74: All use 'c: any' and 's: any' type casting
No validation that c.tier, c.status, c.source exist or have expected values
Filter operations like filter(c => c.status === 'active').length assume status is a string
If Supabase schema changes or corrupted data exists, filters silently fail
Line 76: c.monthly_gmv could be null, NaN, or non-numeric
findings.scenario: Given a corrupted record where creators.status = NULL, when PUT /api/kpi filters by status === 'active', then that creator is excluded from activeCreators count, producing incorrect dashboard metrics.
findings.impact: Silent data quality issues: Dashboard shows incorrect metrics. Tier breakdown counts are inaccurate. No warning to admin.
findings.fix: Add explicit type guards or runtime validation.

findings.title: /api/kpi POST weeks_to_30k calculation may produce NaN or Infinity
trace: Line 24: Checks if body.weekly_net_increase && body.cumulative_affiliates exist
Line 25: remaining = 30000 - body.cumulative_affiliates
Line 26-28: weeks_to_30k = remaining > 0 ? remaining / weekly_net_increase : 0
If body.weekly_net_increase === 0, division by zero produces Infinity
If body.cumulative_affiliates is negative, remaining could be > 30000 and result is huge
No validation that inputs are positive numbers
findings.scenario: Given POST /api/kpi with {weekly_net_increase: 0, cumulative_affiliates: 0}, when weeks_to_30k is calculated, then parseFloat(Infinity.toFixed(1)) produces NaN.
findings.impact: Dashboard displays NaN or Infinity for weeks_to_30k metric. Breaks analytics UI.
findings.fix: Add input validation and guard against division by zero.

findings.title: TikTok Shop HMAC-SHA256 signature uses appSecret as HMAC key instead of parameter
trace: Line 104: baseString = `${appSecret}${path}${sortedParams}${appSecret}`
Line 105-108: HMAC is created with appSecret as the key
TikTok Shop API documentation (not visible) likely expects different signing scheme
Using appSecret in both baseString AND as HMAC key is redundant and non-standard
Comment on line 96 says 'config에 signFn을 주입받아 사용' indicating this is placeholder/untested code
findings.scenario: Given TikTok Shop API expects standard HMAC signing (e.g., HMAC-SHA256(path+sortedParams, appSecret)), when the client sends signature computed as shown, the TikTok API rejects the request as unauthorized.
findings.impact: API integration fails in production. All TikTok Shop data fetches fail. Orders are not synced. Feature is broken.
findings.fix: Verify with TikTok Shop API documentation and adjust signing logic. Likely remove appSecret from baseString.

findings.title: TikTok crawler Apify token exposed in URL parameter (line 81, 89)
trace: Line 81: `${baseUrl}/actor-runs/${runId}?token=${this.config.apiToken}`
Line 89: `${baseUrl}/datasets/${datasetId}/items?token=${this.config.apiToken}`
API token is passed as query parameter instead of Authorization header
Query parameters are logged in server access logs, cached by proxies, exposed in browser history
Best practice: Use Authorization: Bearer header
findings.scenario: Given server access logs, when a developer reviews logs and sees 'GET /actor-runs/123?token=apify_ABC123XYZ', they expose the API token. Anyone with log access can extract the token and make API calls.
findings.impact: Credential exposure: Apify token visible in logs and caches. Attacker could extract token from logs, browser history, or proxy caches and use it to trigger crawls or exhaust credits.

findings.title: Competitor discovery adapter exposes empty estimates and skips qualification stats
trace: Line 141: estimated_gmv is always undefined (comment: 'TikTok Shop 데이터 없으면 추정 불가')
Line 142: already_in_db flag is exposed to client/caller
This means UI or API could reveal which creators are already in database vs new
In discoverShopAffiliates() method (lines 178-191), avg_views and engagement_rate are hardcoded to 0 instead of calculated
findings.scenario: Given a competitor looking at the API response from competitor discovery, when they see already_in_db: true for many creators, they know those creators are already affiliated with Banilaco. When they see already_in_db: false, they can poach new targets.
findings.impact: Competitive intelligence leak: Competitors learn which creators are already contracted. If this data is exposed via API without auth, it's a business intelligence loss.
findings.fix: Don't expose already_in_db flag to untrusted callers. Calculate engagement_rate and avg_views properly. Filter based on discovered vs existing before returning.

findings.title: AfterShip shipping tracker detectCarrier() regex for DHL is too permissive (10 digits matches many formats)
trace: Line 182: DHL detection is /^\d{10}$/ — matches any 10-digit number
FedEx is also /^\d{12}$/ or /^\d{15}$/
There's overlap: a 12-digit all-numbers string could match FedEx before DHL
Actual DHL format is 11-13 digits, not 10
Regex ordering (line 169-184) is USPS, UPS, FedEx, DHL — so 10 digits might incorrectly match FedEx pattern first
findings.scenario: Given a tracking number '123456789012' (12 digits), when detectCarrier() is called, it matches FedEx regex first (line 178) even if it's actually a DHL number.
findings.impact: Low: Incorrect carrier detection. Tracking lookup fails at AfterShip (API returns 'carrier not found'). User sees error, not a security issue, but degrades UX.
findings.fix: Verify actual DHL format, reorder regex checks by specificity, add comments with format sources.

findings.title: Email notification sender leaks API key in logs if Resend fails
trace: Line 58: fetch() call to Resend API with Authorization header containing API key
Line 62-63: If error, console.error() logs the error object which may contain request headers
Line 74: console.error() also logs response.json() which could include error details
If unhandled fetch() error occurs, Error object might include full request/response
findings.scenario: Given Resend API temporarily down, when EmailNotificationSender.sendEmail() is called, console.error() logs response including headers or error details. A log aggregation system (e.g., Datadog, Sentry) captures the log with API key exposed.
findings.impact: Credential leakage: API key exposed in logs. Attacker with log access can extract key and send emails as crew@banilaco.com.
findings.fix: Sanitize error logs, don't log full response, log only status code and message.

findings.title: auto_update_tier trigger fires on every UPDATE, not just GMV changes, causing unnecessary tier recalculation
trace: Lines 325-346: auto_update_tier() trigger fires BEFORE UPDATE on creators
Trigger recalculates tier based on monthly_gmv and monthly_content_count every time any field is updated
Example: Updating 'notes' field triggers tier calculation, even if GMV didn't change
Lines 328-340: Tier logic checks both monthly_gmv and monthly_content_count but no guard to skip if unchanged
This is inefficient (unnecessary DB computations) but not a bug per se, more of a design issue
findings.scenario: Given a creator with tier='gold' and monthly_gmv=2000, when an admin updates the 'notes' field, the trigger recalculates tier even though GMV hasn't changed.
findings.impact: Performance: Trigger fires unnecessarily on every update. Not critical but inefficient. Could cause unexpected tier downgrades if monthly_content_count check is applied incorrectly.
findings.fix: Add guard to only recalculate if monthly_gmv or monthly_content_count changed.

findings.title: /api/creator/recommendations GET/POST/PATCH endpoints lack proper authorization scope validation
trace: Lines 14-30: GET endpoint fetches creator_id from creator_accounts linked to auth user
Lines 62-88: POST endpoint does same lookup and generates recommendations
Lines 109-169: PATCH endpoint verifies recommendation.creator_id matches creator_id from auth user
Authorization chain: auth.uid() -> creator_accounts.auth_user_id -> creator_id -> creator_recommendations.creator_id
If creator_accounts table has a bug or is inconsistent, authorization can fail silently
findings.scenario: Given creator A authenticated, when they PATCH /api/creator/recommendations with someone else's recommendation_id, the code checks if rec.creator_id === account.creator_id (line 147). If the lookup is cached or stale, the check might pass incorrectly.
findings.impact: Medium: Creator A could modify creator B's recommendations if cross-lookup is exploited. However, the code does have the check, so vulnerability is low unless there's a race condition.
findings.fix: The code is mostly secure, but add audit logging for PATCH operations. Consider adding creator_id validation at middleware level.
findings.fix_code:
Add logging to PATCH handler:
```typescript
console.log(`[AUDIT] Creator ${userId} dismissed recommendation ${recommendation_id} (creator_id=${account.creator_id})`);
```

findings.title: RecommendationEngine class imported but not defined (type error)
trace: Line 3: import { RecommendationEngine } from '@/lib/ai/recommendation-engine'
Line 87: const engine = new RecommendationEngine()
File path @/lib/ai/recommendation-engine not provided in review scope
Cannot verify if file exists or if generateRecommendations() method exists
This will fail at runtime if class is not implemented
findings.scenario: Given POST /api/creator/recommendations is called, when RecommendationEngine is instantiated, the import fails because file is missing or class is undefined.
findings.impact: Runtime error: 500 error on POST /api/creator/recommendations. Feature is broken. However, this is a code structure issue, not a security bug.
findings.fix: Verify RecommendationEngine is implemented and exported correctly.
findings.fix_code: Check if file exists at src/lib/ai/recommendation-engine.ts. If not, create stub or implement the class.

findings.title: Middleware skips auth for /api/sync with no token validation
trace: Lines 27-30: /api/sync routes skip middleware auth entirely
Comment says 'Sync API — 별도 Bearer 토큰 인증 (middleware에서 스킵)'
Implies sync routes have their own token validation, but not shown in review scope
If sync endpoints don't validate Bearer token, they're open to unauthorized access
findings.scenario: Given /api/sync routes bypass middleware, if they don't implement proper Bearer token validation, an attacker can call /api/sync/orders without a token.
findings.impact: Potential unauthorized access to sync operations (order sync, shipping sync, etc.). Impact depends on whether sync endpoints validate tokens. Needs verification.
findings.fix: Ensure all /api/sync endpoints validate Bearer token. Add validation at sync endpoint or create custom middleware.

recommendations: Implement consistent auth/authz validation across all API routes. Don't rely on RLS alone.
Replace 'USING (true)' RLS policies with role-based access control (admin, manager, viewer, creator).
Add input validation and field whitelisting to all update operations.
Implement rate limiting on public endpoints using Upstash or similar.
Use Authorization header instead of query parameters for sensitive tokens.
Add audit logging for sensitive operations (update_status, PATCH recommendations, etc.).
Verify TikTok Shop API signing logic against official documentation.
Fix DHL tracking format detection to be more specific.
Sanitize error logs to prevent credential leakage.
Add guards to triggers to prevent unnecessary recalculation.
